coredata.dc:title: Guanidyl-functionalized polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane porous hybrid polymer coating for specific solid phase microextraction of phthalate esters in foodstuff
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ning
Lei, Xiaoyun
Huang, Ting
Su, Lishen
Zhang, Lan
Xie, Zenghong
Wu, Xiaoping
coredata.dc:description: A guanidyl-functionalized polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (G-POSS) porous polymeric hybrid coating was developed for specific solid-phase microextraction (SPME) of phthalate esters (PAEs), via in-situ click photopolymerization on a thiolated stainless steel (SS) fiber. The guanidyl-functionalized l-cysteine (G-Cys) monomer was fast copolymerized with methacryl substituted polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane in the presence of initiator and porogen. The resultant G-POSS hybrid coating exhibited large specific surface, fast mass transfer, and good stability (water- and thermal-resistance). It showed mixed-mode recognition regime (hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interaction and “molecular sieve effect”), and therefore demonstrated great benefits in SPME - gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) analysis of PAEs with low detection limits (0.054–2.51 ng L−1), wide linear range (1.0–3000.0 ng L−1), and appropriate repeatability. The coating was successfully applied for the enrichment and analysis of PAEs in milk and rice samples. This work indicates that the POSS-based functionalized porous hybrid polymer is a promising candidate for the enrichment of complicated samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phthalate esters
Solid-phase microextraction
Gas chromatography–mass spectrometry
G-POSS porous hybrid polymer

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of G-POSS hybrid polymer coated fiber
Preparation of G-POSS hybrid polymer coated fiber
Fabrication and characterization of G-POSS hybrid coating
The stability and durability of the G-POSS coated fiber
Adsorption and extraction characteristics of the G-POSS coated fiber
Enrichment capacity of the G-POSS hybrid polymer coating toward PAEs
Application to analysis of food samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fujian Provincial Department of Science and Technology
Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication of G-POSS hybrid polymer coated SPME fiber and its extraction process.

(a) FTIR spectra of different coatings; (b) XPS spectrum (S2p) of G-POSS; Scanning electron micrographs of (c) bare stainless steel wire (300×), (d) G-POSS-coated fiber (300×) and (e) G-POSS coating with high magnification (3000×); (f) zeta potential of POSS, G-Cys and G-POSS; (g) TGA curves of the G-POSS (the red line) and the G-Cys (the black line) coatings; tests for (h) water-resistant property and (i) thermal-resistant property of G-POSS fiber coatings. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Influence of experimental conditions on the extraction efficiency for the PAEs obtained on the G-POSS-coated fiber. (a) extraction temperature; (b) extraction time; (c) desorption temperature, (d) desorption time; (e) stirring rate and (f) salt concentration. Conditions: sample volume: 20 mL; concentration of analytes: 0.5 ng mL−1.
The nitrogen-adsorption isotherms and pore size distribution of different coatings. (a) POSS; (b) fiber coating 2; (c) fiber coating 5.
Water contact angles of G-Cys (a) and G-POSS hybrid polymer (b).
Selective recognition of G-POSS hybrid coating toward PAEs. Analytes: Ten PAEs (0.5 ng mL−1), butylbenzene (5.0 ng mL−1), phenylpentane (5.0 ng mL−1).
Comparison of the extraction capacity for PAEs obtained by the G-POSS coating with that of the commercial coatings (65 µm PDMS/DVB, 75 µm CAR/PDMS and 100 µm PDMS) (a) and the POSS coating (b).
XPS spectrum of N1s of the G-POSS coating (upper) and the G-POSS coating (below) after treated with PAEs.

Ion chromatograms of PAEs obtained by the (a) milk and (b) rice samples. The black lines: direct injection of blank samples; the blue lines: SPME treated blank samples; and the red lines: spiked samples. Concentration of the analytes: 0.5 ng mL−1. PAEs: 1. DMP; 2. DEP; 3. BAE; 4. DIBP; 5. DBP; 6. DPP; 7. BBP; 8. DEHP; 9. DCHP; 10. DNOP. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Analytical performance for DI-SPME-GC-MS analysis of the PAEs.
Recovery and precision for the analysis of food samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aSpiked with 0.5 ng mL−1 PAEs standard solution.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of current strategy with previously reported methods for PAEs analysis.

row.entry: DMP, DEP, BAE, DIBP, DBP, DPP, BBP, DEHP, DCHP, DNOP
DMP, DEP, DIBP, DNBP, BMEP, BMPP, BEEP, DPP, DNHP, BBP, BBEP, DCHP, BEHP.
DMP, DEP, BBP, DBP, DEHP, DOP

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: DAP: diallyl phthalate; DNBP: di-n-butylphthalate; BMEP: bis(2-methoxyethyl)phthalate; BEEP: bis(2-ethoxyethyl)phthalate.
BMPP: Phthalic acid, bis-4-methyl-2-pentyl ester; DNHP: Phthalic acid, bis-hexyl ester.

head.ce:title: Guanidyl-functionalized polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane porous hybrid polymer coating for specific solid phase microextraction of phthalate esters in foodstuff

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology (Ministry of Education & Fujian Province), PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology (Ministry of Education & Fujian Province)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology (Ministry of Education & Fujian Province), PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Food Safety and Environmental Monitoring, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350116, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Food Safety and Environmental Monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Food Safety and Environmental Monitoring, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350116, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A guanidyl-functionalized polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (G-POSS) porous polymeric hybrid coating was developed for specific solid-phase microextraction (SPME) of phthalate esters (PAEs), via in-situ click photopolymerization on a thiolated stainless steel (SS) fiber. The guanidyl-functionalized l-cysteine (G-Cys) monomer was fast copolymerized with methacryl substituted polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane in the presence of initiator and porogen. The resultant G-POSS hybrid coating exhibited large specific surface, fast mass transfer, and good stability (water- and thermal-resistance). It showed mixed-mode recognition regime (hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interaction and “molecular sieve effect”), and therefore demonstrated great benefits in SPME - gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) analysis of PAEs with low detection limits (0.054–2.51 ng L−1), wide linear range (1.0–3000.0 ng L−1), and appropriate repeatability. The coating was successfully applied for the enrichment and analysis of PAEs in milk and rice samples. This work indicates that the POSS-based functionalized porous hybrid polymer is a promising candidate for the enrichment of complicated samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Phthalate esters (PAEs), a kind of plasticizers widely used in plastics products and food contact material, have been considered as a group of endocrine disruptors [1,2]. As the most highly exposed aromatic chemicals, PAEs can be released easily and migrate to the environment and food chain, exhibiting carcinogenicity or tumorigenicity to humans and wildlife even at low concentration levels [3,4]. Due to their potential threat to environment and human health, some of PAEs have been listed as the priority pollutants by international organizations such as European Union (EU) and United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). A selective and sensitive strategy is critical to enrich and determine PAEs from complicated matrices.

SPME has been recognized as a green sample pretreatment method for the PAEs prior to GC–MS or LC analysis. Currently, the fiber-based SPME is the most commonly used format. The sensitivity and applicability of this technique are greatly relied on the adsorption capacity and the mass transfer of coating materials. However, the commercially available coatings suffer from the drawbacks of limited variety, high cost and low stability. Especially, they identify the target by the polarity, resulting in the loss of selectivity and recovery. Recently, task-specific SPME coatings based on the natural receptors (antibodies [5] and aptamers [6]) and artificial polymers (molecularly imprinted polymers [7,8], polymeric ionic liquids [9,10], restricted access materials [11,12]) have been developed for the enrichment of polar and aromatic pharmaceuticals [5,6,10–12] or phenols [7–9], demonstrating enhanced extraction efficiency and selectivity. These results indicated that surface functionality and rigid porous structure were essential for improving the adsorption capacity and selectivity of coatings. Exploring porous coating materials with controllable affinity is highly desired to enhance the sensitivity of PAEs recognition in SPME.

The key strategy for diversifying the functionality of organic frameworks is the use of rigid building blocks with multiple linking sites [13]. Polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (POSS) is a typical inorganic building block used for the fabrication of porous materials [14], due to its rigid three dimensional cage-type silica nanostructure, reactive organofunctionalized groups and connectable structure. It can be easily incorporated into organic polymers and results in dramatic improvement in polymer properties. With respect to the preparation of fiber coating, POSS has recently been used as the vinylic cross-linker in the copolymerization, showing the capability in increasing mechanical stability [15], porosity and surface area of the polymer [16]. It is suggested that a functionalized porous network with higher chemical/framework stability and desired affinity, can be constructed by further combining POSS units with functional organic monomers in polymerization.

The guanidyl functional group has emerged as a very interesting topic in supermolecular and coordination chemistry, due to its intrinsic propensity to form strong hydrogen bonds with phosphate esters, carboxylates and metal ions [17]. It can be easily protonated (pKa 13.6) and show special acid/base properties [18]. The specific Y-shaped structure of the protonated guanidyl allows delocalization of the electrons, resulting in triple resonance stability and two opposite interactions including cation–cation stacking and strong hydrogen bonds with polar molecules. As a hydrogen bond driven event, guanidyl-based binding is often enthalpically favorable and provides theepitopes a balance of favorable attraction [19]. This event has been widely used in the design of selective binders for anionic targets or capturers for CO2 or intact phosphorylated proteins [20], which features promising application in catalysis, sensor and sample treatment. These unique characters greatly inspire us to utilize guanidyl-based molecular recognition in the development of functionalized coatings for effective recognition of PAEs.

Herein, we prepared a guanidyl-functionalized polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane porous hybrid polymer (G-POSS) coating on the stainless steel wire via direct photopolymerization for the first time, and applied it in the SPME of PAEs in food stuffs. The resultant porous polymer coatings showed superior extraction efficiency and stability for recognizing PAEs as compared to commercial fiber coatings. This can be attributed to the introduction of guanidyl functional groups as the specific acceptors for PAEs on the coating. Recognition between the target and the coating was executed by forming stable eight-membered ring through strong hydrogen bonding and π stacking. Besides, the rigid skeleton of nano-sized POSS endowed the prepared hybrid polymer with high chemical and thermal stability, large specific surface and hydrophobic sites for PAEs adsorption. The bimodal porous structure of hybrid coating enables the extraction/desorption process to be fast and controllable. Finally, we used the G-POSS hybrid coating for the direct immerse extraction of trace PAEs from milk and rice prior to their GC–MS analysis.

Methacryl substituted polyhedral oligomericsilsesquioxane reagent (cage mixture, n = 8, 10, 12; POSS) was purchased from Hybrid Plastic, Inc. (Hattiesburg, MS, USA). Polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG200) was purchased from Sigma (USA). 2,2-Dimethoxy-2-phenylacetophenone (DMPA) was purchased from J&K Chemical (Beijing, China). l-cysteine, 2-ethyl-2-thio-pseudourehy-drobromide and the PAEs (listed in Table S1, including DMP, DEP, BAE, DIBP, DBP, DPP, BBP, DEHP, DCHP, DNOP) were all obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Other reagents were at least of analytical grade and list in the Supporting information. Ultrapure water used throughout was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). The stock solution of PAEs (1000 µg mL−1 in N-hexane) was stored at 4 °C in darkness. Working solutions were freshly prepared by diluting the stock solution with acetone.

PAEs analysis was performed on a QP2020 GC–MS system (Shimadzu, Japan) using a Shimadu SH-Rxi-5SIL MS capillary column (30 m length × 0.25 mm i.d. × 0.25 μm film thickness) in electron impact ionization (EI) mode with an ionizing energy of 70 eV. High-purity helium (99.999%) was used as the carrier gas at a constant flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. The injector temperature was set at 280 °C. The GC oven temperature program was set as follows: 50 °C for 1 min; 20 °C min−1 to 220 °C for 1 min; then 5 °C min−1 to 280 °C for 5 min. Split injections (20:1) were used throughout. The interface temperature and the ion source temperature were set at 250 °C and 230 °C, respectively. Data acquisition was performed in the selected ion monitoring (SIM) mode in which m/z was listed in Table S1.

The polymerization was executed in a Spectrolinker™ XL-1500 UV cross-linker (λ = 365 nm, Spectronics Corporation, USA). The infrared (IR) spectrum was recorded by Nicolet Avatar 330 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250, Thermo) was used to examine the surface elemental composition of the coating. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was measured on an ASAP 2460 nitrogen adsorption apparatus (Micromeritics, Atlanta, USA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were acquired by a Nova NanoSEM 230 instrument (FEI, USA). The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) experiments were performed on a STA449C/6/G thermal gravimetric analyzer (Netzsch, Bavaria, Germany) from room temperature to 600 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of G-POSS hybrid polymer coated fiber

ce:section.ce:para: Guanidyl-functionalized l-cysteine (G-Cys) monomer was synthesized according to the reported procedure [20]. First, 3.5 g 2-ethyl-2-thio-pseudourehydrobromide was dissolved in 6.5 mL mixed solvent of DMSO and THF (VDMSO:VTHF = 3.5:3.0), and stirred for 20 min at room temperature. Subsequently, l-cysteine (2.3 g) was added in solution slowly at 0 °C, and then the mixture was transferred to room temperature and kept covered for 26 h under continuous stirring. The resulting G-Cys product was washed with methanol for six times, collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min, and then dried overnight in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. SEM, TGA and XPS were used to characterize the synthesized G-Cys monomer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of G-POSS hybrid polymer coated fiber
ce:section.ce:para: The G-POSS hybrid polymer coated fibers were synthesized by an in-situ photopolymerization approach, and the fabrication of the G-POSS hybrid coating involved the following processes (Fig. 1 ). (a) The stainless steel wire (φ 0.30 mm; Gaoge Industrial and Trade Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was first treated with hydrofluoric acid and water, and coated by silver layer via silver mirror reaction following the reported procedure [21]. It was then immersed in 20.0 mM of 1,6-hexanedithiol for 24 h self-assembly. After water washing and air drying, a -SH functionalized SS fiber was obtained for further use. (b) For photopolymerization of the G-POSS hybrid coating, a polymerization mixture was prepared in centrifugal tube by ultrasonically mixing G-Cys (20 mg), POSS (50 mg), PEG 200 (100 mg) and n-propanol (80 mg) for 10 min. 20 mg DMPA was then added into the mixture under an ultrasonic bath for another 3 min to ensure completely mixing. The polymerization mixture solution was freshly made before each coating. (c) The thiol functionalized SS fiber was carefully dipped into the polymerization mixture solution and pulled out quickly to form a thin layer of hybrid coating. Subsequently, it was placed into the UV cross-linker for in situ photopolymerization for 2.0 min to complete the chemical bonding between thiol and vinyl. The UV curing process was repeated three times to obtain a coating with sufficient thickness. The obtained fiber was carefully rinsed with methanol for three times, fixed on a SPME fiber holder and then conditioned in the GC injector under helium overnight at 250 °C. The thermal stability of prepared G-POSS hybrid coating was evaluated by TGA.

Various mass ratio of G-Cys monomer, cross-linker (POSS), porogen (PEG 200, n-propanol) and initiator (DMPA) (WG-Cys:WPOSS:WPEG 200:Wn-propanol = 2:5:7:11, 2:5:10:8, 2:5:11:7, 5:9:14:22, 5:9:20:16 and 5:9:22:14, w%) were used to synthesize different G-POSS hybrid coatings for optimization of preparation, which were defined as G-POSS1 (fiber 1), G-POSS2 (fiber 2), G-POSS3 (fiber 3), G-POSS4 (fiber 4), G-POSS5 (fiber 5), and G-POSS6 (fiber 6). The composition and definition of each coating were listed in Table S2.

The G-POSS-coated fiber was immersed into the PAEs working solution (100 ng mL−1) or real sample solution directly to perform the enrichment in a 50 °C thermostatic water bath, under stirring at the rate of 1000 rpm. The ionic strength was controlled by adding NaCl (w/v, 5%) into the solution. After extracting for 30 min, the fiber was removed from the vial and immediately inserted into a GC inlet for desorption at 280 °C for 6 min, followed by the GC–MS analysis.

The commercially available SPME fibers including polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS, 100 μm), polydimethylsiloxane/divinylbenzene (PDMS/DVB, 65 μm), and carboxen/polydimethylsiloxane (CAR/PDMS, 75 μm) (Supelco, 57324-U, USA) were used for the comparison experiments. Before daily use, all the fibers need to be conditioned in the GC injection port at 250 °C for 1 h.

The milk and rice samples were obtained from the local supermarket (Fuzhou, China). 20.0 mL milk sample was treated by adding NaAc (0.4 g), MgSO4 (0.8 g), and acetonitrile (8.0 mL) into centrifuge vial for protein precipitation, followed by a centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and stored at 4 °C for further extraction.

The grinded rice (5 g) was extracted by ultrasonic method with 2.5 g NaCl, 20.0 mL water and 5.0 mL acetone for 30 min. The mixture was then added with 10 mL hexane and vortexed for 1 min, followed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was collected, and the precipitant was treated again according to the above method. All the supernatant were mixed and purged to dryness with nitrogen flow. The dry residue was redissolved in 5 mL acetonitrile and diluted with water to 200 mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of G-POSS hybrid coating
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic design of the fiber coating for PAEs is shown in Fig. 1. The G-Cys monomer was prepared via a nucleophilic substitution reaction of S-ethylisothiourea hydrobromide and l-cysteine. Subsequently, the G-POSS hybrid polymer coating was obtained by using a thiol-ene photopolymerization of the G-Cys and the POSS. The vinyl POSS was used as a crosslinker for the covalent bonding of the G-Cys monomer on the SS fiber, as well as the interparticle bridging by vinyl self-polymerization, as confirmed by XPS and FT-IR analysis. The entirely covalent-linked network endowed the resultant coating with enhanced stability and durability.

An initial optimization of coating procedure was performed by changing the ratio of monomer, cross-linker and porogen in the polymerization mixture during the fabrication of G-POSS hybrid polymer. As listed in Table S2 and Fig. S1 (Electronic Supporting information), fiber 2 with monomer/crosslinker/porogen ratios of 2/5/18 was selected as the optimal coating for further characterization, according to the relative peak area of each analyte.

Fig. 2 a represents the FT-IR spectrum of G-POSS hybrid coating, in which the adsorption bands at 745, 1100, 1300, 1409, 1586 and 1640 cm−1 were assigned to the CSC [22], SiOSi [23–25], CO [26], CN, CN and CC [22] stretching vibration of polymer, respectively. The disappearance of the SH stretching vibration at 2552 cm−1 [22] and the new adsorption band of CSC stretching vibration at 745 cm−1 [22] revealed the thiol-ene reaction between the thiol groups of G-Cys or the pretreated SS fiber and the vinyls on POSS, leading to the covalent bridging. Meanwhile, the presence of the other bands (especially Si–OSi [23–25], CN and CN) and the weakening of CC [22] adsorption suggested the successfully incorporation of POSS skeleton and guadine group, and the interparticle bridging of POSS as well. All the vital linkages were attributed to the formation of robust multilayer coating on the SS fiber.

To further identify the successful preparation of G-Cys monomer and elemental composition of hybrid coating, the XPS analysis was conducted. The full survey spectrum of G-Cys monomer (Fig. S2a) shows signals from C, N, O and S elements. The spectrum of the N1s (Fig. S2b) that can be fitted into three peaks (NH2, NH and CNH) demonstrates the successful preparation of monomer containing guanidyl groups. The full survey spectrum of hybrid coating (Fig. S3) shows signals from C, N, O, S and Si elements, indicating the incorporation of G-Cys and POSS monomer into the hybrid polymer. The spectrum of the S2p (Fig. 2b) can be fitted into three peaks: S2p3/2 of Ag–S bond at 162.6 eV [27], the S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 of the CSC covalent bond at 163.7 and 164.9 eV, respectively [28]. These results provided clear evidence that the Ag layer on the SS fiber was thiolated by 1,6-hexanedithiol, and the vinyl groups of POSS cross linked with the -SH groups on the G-Cys monomers and the surface of the fiber via thiol-ene reaction. The higher mass ratio of the S and N elements (43:33) in XPS spectra, as compared to the data (32:42) obtained in the case of single reaction, also confirmed the presence of two thiol-ene chemical processes. The presence of C–S–C bond indicated a vital covalent linking of POSS with G-Cys and thiolated SS fibers, which greatly improve the stability and functionality of fibers.

The morphology of as-prepared hybrid coating was investigated by SEM. Fig. 2c reveals the surface of bare etched SS fiber with an approximate diameter of 0.22 mm. After thiolation and photopolymerization, a homogenous, porous and dense G-POSS coating was observed on the surface of the SS fiber (Fig. 2d, e). As shown in Fig. 2e, the obtained porous structure (throughpore, mesopore and micropore) of the coating materials would facilitate the solute mass transfer and the extraction process. The thickness of the hybrid coating can be adjusted by varying the coating time. An optimal coating time of 6 min was chosen to synthesize the G-POSS coating with a thickness of 40.1 μm for PAEs extraction, as indicated in Fig. 2c and d.

To assess the surface charges and particle dispersion of hybrid coating, zeta potential measurements were conducted for different materials including G-Cys monomer, POSS, and G-POSS hybrid polymer. As shown in Fig. 2f, a negative net zeta potential value of 9.13 mV was obtained with the G-Cys monomer, due to an integrated result of various charged groups. Compared to the zeta potential of POSS (−7.86 mV), a negative increase of the zeta potential of G-POSS (−14.1 mV), that can be attributed to a conversion to a zwitterionic surface, demonstrated bonding between the G-Cys and POSS. The high stability of G-POSS hybrid polymer was evidenced by its high absolute value of 14.1 mV comparing to that of G-Cys and POSS, since the nanoparticle composites can be considered to be stable when their absolute value of zeta potential are in the range of 5–22 mV [29]. This result reveals the presence of a stable chemical bond between G-Cys and POSS, which was consistent with the results of FTIR and XPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability and durability of the G-POSS coated fiber
ce:section.ce:para: The robustness of hybrid coating was evaluated by conducting a series of measurements including the thermal assays, zeta potential and chemical stability tests. As shown in the TGA of G-POSS (Fig. 2g), a weight loss of 17% was obtained before 230 °C, due to the loss of adsorbed moisture of the polymer. Whilst a long plateau was maintained till a weight loss of about 20 wt% was observed at 380 °C, implying the decomposition of the monomer covalently bonded on the surface of POSS [30]. The result demonstrated an excellent thermal stability of the G-POSS coating, which well fits the requirement for GC–MS application. As shown in Fig. 2g, it is worth noting that the thermal stability of G-POSS coating was greatly enhanced by using the POSS as cross-linker, as compared to that of the G-Cys coating, owing to the incorporation of rigid silica cage of POSS that can inhibit the mutual dissociation and degradation of surrounding molecules [31] and the formation of multilayer cross-linking networks as well.

The chemical stability of hybrid coatings was evaluated by comparing their extraction efficiencies at different chemical conditions. The extraction efficiency obtained at the optimal condition was taken as 100%. Water- and thermal-resistance tests were conducted by immersing the G-POSS-coated fibers in water for different time interval (0 to 48 h) or for 3 h at different temperatures (260 °C, 280 °C and 300 °C). Fig. 2h and i showed that the RSDs for extraction efficiencies at different immerse time or under various temperature were all below 10% for PAEs. The satisfied chemical stability of as-prepared G-POSS hybrid coating could be attributed to the good corrosion-resistance of POSS skeleton [32] and the robust covalent bonded network. This feature is of great benefit for the improvement of fiber lifetime in DI-SPME. As shown in Fig. S4a, the G-POSS coated fiber can be used repeatedly at least for 200 extraction/desorption cycles without considerable change of the extraction efficiency. The durability of the fiber was also verified by the FTIR of the coating obtained after 200 uses (Fig. S4b), in which the characteristic peaks of the coating remained the same as their original forms (Fig. 2a). These results suggested that the interparticle bridging of rigid silica cage of POSS and the covalent linking between G-POSS and SS fiber contributed to the outstanding stability and durability of hybrid coating.

It is well known that the diffusion rate of the analyte increase with the increased extraction temperature, which is beneficial to shorten the balance time of targets. However, excessive temperature accompanies by a smaller partition coefficient of the analyte, resulting in a lower extraction amount on the coating. The effect of extraction temperature on the extraction amount was studied from 45 °C to 55 °C. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the peak areas of all analytes achieved the highest within 50 °C due to the fast diffusion and then slightly decreased due to the decrease of partition coefficient at excessive temperature. So, the extraction temperature was set at 50 °C. SPME is an equilibrium-based extraction procedure and therefore a time-dependent process. The extraction time from 25 to 35 min was studied (Fig. 3b), and 30 min was suitable for nearly all PAEs to keep distribution equilibrium in the coatings and in the sample solution, and thus selected as the optimal extraction time.

Proper desorption temperature and time need to be optimized for releasing sufficiently PAEs from coating. Increasing the desorption temperature led to a decrease in the partition coefficient of the targets between the coating and the gaseous phase, minimizing desorption time and carry-over. However, excessive temperature may damage the coating and result in a shortened lifetime. To achieve the maximum desorption of the analyte from the coating, the effect of desorption temperature was investigated. Fig. 3c showed that peak areas increased steadily as desorption temperature increased from 265 °C to 280 °C. Further increasing the desorption temperature led to the reduction of the peak areas. Desorption time was evaluated from 3 to 7 min. Fig. 3d showed that the peak areas of the PAEs increased gradually with the increase of desorption time from 3 to 6 min, and then kept stable. Therefore, 280 °C for 6 min was chosen as the optimal desorption conditions for further experiments.

The stirring speed and ionic strength are important parameters that can influence the mass diffusion and distribution of analytes between the sample solution and the fiber in the extraction process. The stirring speed from 750 to 1250 rpm was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3e, highest peak areas for PAEs were obtained when the stirring speed was set at 1000 rpm, due to the accelerated mass transfer of analytes. Further studies revealed that peak areas of the PAEs increased with the increase of ionic strength from 0 to 5%, and reached a maximum at 5% (Fig. 3f). This phenomenon can be explained by an increase of the solubility of analytes occurred when the salt was added, which lead to a variation in the partition coefficient of analytes between the fiber coating and the solution, and therefore change the extraction efficiency. The ionic strength was set at 5% in further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and extraction characteristics of the G-POSS coated fiber
ce:section.ce:para: N2 adsorption/desorption analysis can provide a direct insight to examine the porous structure of polymer materials. To elucidate the interaction between guanidyl functional group and the pore structure, adsorption isotherms of two G-POSS hybrid polymers synthesized with different mass ratio of G-Cys and POSS were compared to that of POSS, as listed in Fig. 4 and Table S3. All the adsorption–desorption isotherms of hybrid coatings can be classified as a type IV isotherm with a H4-type hysteresis loop, demonstrating a micropore and mesopore structure. It was note worthing that the pore sizes of the G-POSS hybrid polymers were nearly the same as that of the POSS, which indicates that the tunnel of polymers was not occupied by the guanidyl functional group. This result revealed a repulsion of hydrophilic guanidyl group by the hydrophobic interparticle bridging of POSS skeleton, i.e., the periphery of the tunnel, which will be benefit for the specific capture of PAEs by the functionalized recognition sites on the surface of POSS hybrid. The highest BET surface area (258.2 m2/g) and the pore volume (0.42 cm3/g) of G-POSS hybrid coating were obtained on the fiber 2, which was in accordance with the result for extraction performance. Fig. 4 also indicated a pore size distribution from 1.7 to 4.1 nm for the G-POSS hybrid network with two average pore sizes of 1.74 nm and 4.01 nm, confirming the presence of micropore and mesopore structure in the material. These results suggested a lower degree of cross-linking in the G-POSS hybrid network, possibly due to the use of rapid photopolymerization, which can offer more guanidyl groups to form inter- or intra- molecular hydrogen bonds and thus result in the decrease in porosity. On another aspect, the presence of abundant guanidyl binding sites on the surface would benefit the adsorption of PAEs molecules.

The hydrophobicity of the G-POSS coating was evaluated by exploring water contact angle through a sessile drop on specimen. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, the water contact angle of G-Cys and G-POSS hybrid were 28.8° and 132.6°, respectively, indicating that high hydrophobicity is the dominating force of the POSS–based hybrid material, even when it bonds with hydrophilic guanidyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enrichment capacity of the G-POSS hybrid polymer coating toward PAEs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the recognition specificity, the G-POSS hybrid polymer coating was applied to enrich PAEs from the mixture solution of PAEs and noncarboxylated benzenes, butylbenzene (BBZ) and phenylpentane (PLT), in concentration ratios of 1:10. As shown in Fig. 6 , the relative abundances of most of PAEs were drastically increased, while those of carboxylated benzenes were unchanged after extraction, revealing the high specificity of G-POSS coating toward PAEs. It is observed that the enrichment of this coating for the hydrophilic DMP molecule was not obvious, confirming that the hydrophobic interaction is prevail in the SPME.

To assess the enrichment performance of the G-POSS-coated fiber, a comparison experiment on the PAEs extraction was performed, and illustrated in Fig. 7 a. Results showed that the G-POSS coating exhibit significantly higher extraction performance toward PAEs than its commercial counterparts, i.e., the commonly used polymer- or hybrid polymer-based fiber coatings such as PDMS, CAR/PDMS and PDMS/DVB, under the same conditions. Its superiority in adsorption affinity for PAEs might be attributed to the multiple interactions between the G-POSS coating and the analytes, including hydrogen bonding between the guanidyl group of coating and the carboxylic ester group of PAEs, π–π interaction of the aromatic rings of the analytes with the π-conjugated groups of the coating, and “molecular sieve effect” to the PAEs.

To further confirm the specific recognition of the G-POSS coating toward PAEs, the extraction efficiency of as-prepared coating was compared with that of the POSS coating under the same fabrication and extraction conditions. As indicated in Fig. 7b, the POSS coating did play a role in enriching PAEs, owing to its hydrophobicity and π stacking interaction with PAEs. Obviously, the G-POSS-coated fiber showed a higher extraction capacity for PAEs than POSS-coated fiber. This superiority might be attributed to the strong hydrogen bonding between the G-POSS coatings and the PAEs. The result was evidenced by the comparison of N1s spectra from XPS between two coatings. As shown in Fig. 8 , a positive shift of peaks of –NH (400.0 eV) and –NH2 (401.7 eV) was observed in the G-POSS coating after treated with PAEs, as compared to those of G-POSS coating (399.5 eV [33] and 401.0 eV [34], respectively), indicating the presence of molecular interaction of guanidyl-based coating with PAEs. Interestingly, the peak at 401.7 eV belonged to the “graphitic” N or the element in the ring [35], proving the formation of stable eight-membered rings between the guanidyl groups of G-POSS coating and the ester groups of PAEs via hydrogen bonding (Fig. S5).

These results indicated that the G-POSS hybrid coating possessed remarkable enrichment performance for PAEs. The high selectivity can be ascribed to the multiple interactions between the coating and the target analytes: (1) A strong hydrogen bonding interaction between the guanidyl group of hybrid coating and the ester group of PAEs dominates the specific enrichment for PAEs. (2) The hydrophobic surface of G-POSS hybrid coating affords PAEs a “hydrophobic–hydrophobic” interaction. Meanwhile, the π–π interaction between the π-conjugated groups of the hybrid coating and the aromatic rings of PAEs also contributes to the enhancement of adsorption affinity. (3) Owing to the cage-like nature of POSS framework, G-POSS coating imparts a “molecular sieve” effect on PAEs. The micropore size (1.74 nm) of porous network of coatings fits well with the dynamic diameter of PAEs (Table S1), resulting in the sufficiently retention of analytes in the micropores, whilst the mesoporous structure benefits the fast mass transport of the analytes. Therefore, an obvious enhancement in enrichment performance and specificity can be observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to analysis of food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed enrichment method using G-POSS coating was further coupled to GC–MS and applied for the analysis of PAEs residues in food samples. To validate the analytical performance, a series of experiments have been performed under the optimized conditions (Table 1 ). The linear ranges were found to be in the range of 1.0–3000.0 ng L−1. The detection limits (LODs) and limits of quantitation (LOQs) defined as a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 and 10, were in the range of 0.054–2.51 ng L−1 and 0.18–8.37 ng L−1, respectively. The obtained LOQs are well below the maximum admissible concentration of PAEs in drinking water and foods or food simulants, recommended by the EPA [36] and EU [37], respectively. The repeatability of one fiber, i.e., relative standard deviations (RSD), was evaluated to be less than 6.2% (n = 6). The RSDs (n = 3) for fiber-to-fiber were lower than 10%.

The proposed quantitative method was applied to the analysis of trace PAEs in real samples. As shown in Fig. 9 a and b, DIBP, DCHP and DEP, DBP, DPP, DEHP could be detected from the milk and rice samples by this method, respectively. It was satisfactory that the effect of sample matrices on the chromatogram was largely eliminated by applying SPME enrichment, as compared to the unextracted blank samples in Fig. 9. To evaluate the accuracy of the proposed method, the sample was spiked with the PAEs standards of 0.5 ng mL−1 and the recoveries were measured using the standard addition method. The results in Fig. 9 and Table 2 showed that the recoveries of PAEs in milk and rice samples were ranged from 83.4 to 116.5%, with RSDs less than 9.0%. These results indicated the proposed method was quite sensitive and reliable for the determination of trace PAEs from food samples.

A comparison of the current and previously reported methods for the analysis of PAEs was summarized in Table 3 , in terms of coating materials, samples, and analytical characters. It is obviously that the proposed method showed higher sensitivity, lower detection limit, and comparable linear range, recovery and RSDs. Moreover, ten analytes can be detected from food sample by this method, exhibiting the superiority in number of targets and sample complexity. It is expected that the G-POSS porous hybrid polymer material has a great potential for PAEs residual analysis from complicated samples.

Herein we report the first design of a guanidyl-functionalized porous hybrid polymer as the specific enrichment medium for sample preparation. A self-aggregation strategy combined with thiol-ene chemistry was developed for the fabrication of G-POSS hybrid coating with bimodal porous structure and robust multilayer covalent bridging on the SS fibers. Benefitting from the cage-like skeleton and rigid porous structure, the resultant coating demonstrated better thermal/chemical stability and durability, as well as faster mass transfer, thus leading to a shorter analysis time and reliable detection results. The coating was utilized for the DI-SPME of ten PAEs prior to GC–MS analysis, and exhibited higher sensitivity and selectivity over commercial fiber coatings. This superiority can be attributed to the multiple interactions (stable hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interaction, π–π interaction) and matched pore size between the guanidyl-functionalized coating and PAEs, as well as high surface area of porous material. The established method was applied for the analysis of trace PAEs in real agro-products with satisfactory recoveries. This work provides a simple and efficient pathway for preparing the tailorable porous polymers for SPME. The ability to recognize PAEs from complicated food samples offered the proposed SPME-GC–MS method a promising potential for residual monitoring and assessment of PAEs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21375019), the Major Project of Science and Technology of Fujian Province (Grant No. 2018YZ0002-1) and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University of China (Grant No. IRT15R11). We also thank the support from the Collaborative Innovation Center of Chinese Oolong Tea Industry-Collaborative Innovation Center (2011) of Fujian Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.124003.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
